coredata.dc:title: A reverse-signal response fluorescence sensor based on NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and silicon-doped carbon quantum dots for selective determination of copper ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jingjing
Li, Zhixiang
Li, Xiaoqi
Du, Yingxiang
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescence sensor was developed based on NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and silicon-doped carbon quantum dots for the analysis of copper ions. Copper ions enhanced the fluorescence intensity of doped silicon-carbon quantum dots and decreased the intensity of NH2-MIL-101(Fe). The addition of copper ions resulted in fluorescence resonance energy transfer, which caused a reverse change in the fluorescence signal. The sensor based on the above mechanism can eliminate false positive interference. The fluorescence sensing system had a remarkable linear range of 0.25–5 µM for copper ions and a detection limit of 11 nM. It was then applied to water samples, resulting in recoveries ranging from 95.43 % to 103.1 %. The successful construction of this optical sensor not only broadened the way to detect copper ions but also provided a new idea for the detection of other targets mediated by copper ions. Finally, an attempt was made to construct a smartphone-assisted colourimetric platforms to detect copper ions in daylight.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper ions
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots
Metal-organic frameworks
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Cu2+ in actual samples
Synthesis and characterization of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and Si-CQDs
Optical properties of Si-CQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe)
Performance of the sensor for Cu2+ detection
Analysis of copper ion in actual samples
Detection of copper ions by smartphone

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), (d) SEM image of NH2-MIL-101(Fe). (b) TEM image of Si-CQDs (inset is the size distribution of CQDs). (c). XRD spectra of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), (e–h) the elemental mappings of C, N, O and Fe.

(a) The FT-IR spectra of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (blue curve), 1,4-BDC-NH2(red curve), Si-CQDs (black curve). (b) XPS full scan spectrum of Si-CQDs, high-resolution spectra of (c) C1s (d) N1s, (e) O1s and (f) Si 2p.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of (a) Si-CQDs and (b) NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (inset: photographs of Si-CQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) under daylight and 365 nm UV light). Fluorescence emission spectra of (c) NH2-MIL-101(Fe), Si-CQDs, and (d) NH2-MIL-101(Fe) + Si-CQDs in the absence and presence of 2 μM Cu2+ (excitation wavelength = 370 nm).

(a) Fluorescence response of the probe in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+. (b) F563/F433: the ratio of fluorescence intensity at emission wavelengths of 563 and 433 nm. The Linear fitting curves of F563/F433 values versus Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.25 to 5 µM (error amount: 5%) (condition: pH 6.5, 10 mM phosphate buffer, excitation Wavelength = 370 nm, temperature = 80℃).

(a) Selectivity of the probe (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/Si-CQDs) (towards Cu2+ (3 µM) and interfering ions (20 µM) (error amount: 5 %). (b) The stability of the probe in 6 days (error amount: 5 %). (c) The fluorescence ratios of the probe in high salt solution (error amount: 5 %) (condition: pH 6.5, 10 mM phosphate buffer, excitation Wavelength = 370 nm, temperature = 80℃).

The NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/Si-CQDs system was established. The variations of the system before and after the addition of copper ions were evaluated in various aspects. (a) Fluorescence spectrum and UV–Vis spectrum (b) Fluorescence intensity (c) Fluorescence decay curve (d) Zeta potential (condition: pH 6.5, 10 mM phosphate buffer, excitation Wavelength = 370 nm, temperature = 80℃) (error amount: 5%).
(a) Schematic diagram of colorimetric method for detecting copper ions by a smartphone. (b) Linear fitting curve of In (B) versus copper ion concentration (error amount: 5%).
Schematic diagram of the synthesis method of Si-CQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and ratiometric sensor construction.
Comparison of different Fluorescence methods toward Cu2+.
Spiked recoveries for Cu2+ estimated by the probe (n = 3).

row.entry:
Initial Cu2+ amount
determined by the probe (µM)

head.ce:title: A reverse-signal response fluorescence sensor based on NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and silicon-doped carbon quantum dots for selective determination of copper ions

Li), Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (Ministry of Education), China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China (Y. Du).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Hospital of Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence sensor was developed based on NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and silicon-doped carbon quantum dots for the analysis of copper ions. Copper ions enhanced the fluorescence intensity of doped silicon-carbon quantum dots and decreased the intensity of NH2-MIL-101(Fe). The addition of copper ions resulted in fluorescence resonance energy transfer, which caused a reverse change in the fluorescence signal. The sensor based on the above mechanism can eliminate false positive interference. The fluorescence sensing system had a remarkable linear range of 0.25–5 µM for copper ions and a detection limit of 11 nM. It was then applied to water samples, resulting in recoveries ranging from 95.43 % to 103.1 %. The successful construction of this optical sensor not only broadened the way to detect copper ions but also provided a new idea for the detection of other targets mediated by copper ions. Finally, an attempt was made to construct a smartphone-assisted colourimetric platforms to detect copper ions in daylight.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ions are ubiquitous heavy metal ions [1]. The prominent feature of heavy metal ions is the difficulty to degrade, high migration rate, and strong enrichment. Because of the rapid development of modern industry, increasedindustrial activities such as copper-zinc ore mining and smelting, metal processing, mechanical manufacturing, and steel production have resulted in the contamination of copper ions in the river [2]. Copper ions are easy to gather in large quantities in fish and shrimp. Excessive copper ions enter the human body through the food chain, resulting in significant health hazards [1]. Copper ions can interact strongly in the human body and protein and various enzymes, making them lose activity and may also be enriched in certain organs of the human body [3]. Consequently, it is essential to develop an analysis method to detect Cu2+ for environmental safety monitoring.

At present, the most common techniques used for the selective detection method of copper ions includespectrophotometry [4], Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy [5], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy [6]. Desirable analytical performance can be achieved by those conventional methods. However, there are still limitations to these approaches including expensive instrumentation and time-consuming pretreatment. In contrast, fluorescent sensing has outstanding advantages including portability, cheap, visualization [7]. Goswami et al. constructed a “tun-off” fluorescent probe to achieve visual sensing of copper ions [8]. Goswami et al. also employed a rhodamine-benzimidazole conjugate to measure the content of copper ions [20]. Kumar et al. constructed a new 1,8-diaminonaphthalene fluorescence sensor to measure the content of copper ions [17]. Goswami et al. employed fluorescent probes for sensitive bioimaging of copper ions [18,19].

Carbon quantum dots are an excellent fluorescent nanomaterial [9]. There are many advantages of carbon quantum dots including extensive sources of synthetic raw materials, low preparation costs, and rich surface officials [10]. Carbon quantum dots are already widely applied to in vivo and in vitro imaging, sensing, and biomedicine [11]. When different elements are doped into carbon quantum dots, the surface state of the carbon quantum dot will be altered, resulting in a change in fluorescence properties [12]. Silicon-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQDs) are carbon quantum dots doped by silicon elements. Liu et al. employed Si-CQDs to structure a new platform to measure the content of hydroquinone [13].

Metal-organic framework material is a porous crystal material [14]. It is a network structure crystal made of metal ions with organic ligands. NH2-MIL-101 (Fe), as an eminent metal–organic framework material, has been used to build fluorescent sensors due to its excellent properties [15]. The fluorescent strength of a single fluorescent group is easily affected by various factors including temperature, solvent, and concentration [7]. Therefore, there will inevitably be some errors in detecting the target with a single fluorescent group [16]. To solve this problem, considerable effort has been devoted to build ratiometric fluorescent sensors by using two fluorescent signals. When they are affected by other factors, the two fluorescent groups can correct themselves to achieve the purpose of eliminating errors and accurately detecting the content of the target.

In recent years, the widespread use of smartphones has promoted intelligence and digitalization of sensing systems [21]. Li et al. constructed a smart sensing platform to measure the content of copper ions [22]. Liu et al. designed two fluorescence probes (RH-1, RH-2) and combined them with smartphones to develop a simple on-site analysis of copper ions using 3D printing technology [23]. Eda Erdemir et al. used gold nanoparticles to measure the content of copper ions by smartphone [35]. Mehmet Kaplan et al. developed a smartphone-assisted colorimetric method for the detection of copper ions [34].

However, among many sensors that have been designed for the detection of copper ions, the majority of them derive from fluorescence quenching. As a result, these sensors are subject to severe false-positive interference. A fluorescent dual-signal response sensor based on enhancement mechanism was developed in this paper to detect copper ions for the purpose of improving the accuracy of the sensor.

In this work, a novel fluorescence sensor based on blue-emission NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and yellow-emission Si-CQDs has been reported to detect copper ions. The sensor exhibited a dual-fluorescence response to copper ions, whereas the target response in most previously reported papers involved single fluorescence quenching. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the Si-CQDs were generated by the reaction of o-phenylenediamine and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane under high temperature conditions and the NH2-MIL-101 (Fe) was generated by the reaction of ferric chloride and 2-aminoterephthalic acid. Copper ions enhanced the yellow fluorescence peaks of Si-CQDs at 563 nm and decreased the blue fluorescence peaks of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) at 433 nm under 370 nm excitation. There was a remarkable linear relationship within the range of 0.25–5 µM. This method was used to detect the copper ion content in the water sample, and the recovery rate was satisfactory. Furthermore, the RGB value of the reaction solution was also read by smartphone, and the detection of copper ions was successfully achieved.

amino terephthalic acid (2-NH2-1,4-BDC) were supplied by Macklin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China)0.3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES), o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide was gained from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Vancomycin, and Lincomycin were purchased from Jianglai Co., Ltd.

All fluorescence spectrum data were obtained by a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. Zeta potential variations of the sensors were studied with a Malvern laser particle size analyzer. The pH data were acquired by a pHS-25 digital pH meter. Si-CQDs were characterized with a JEM-1400 TEM instrument. UV–visible spectral detection was performed on a UV-1800 spectrophotometer. The composition of the quantum dot was studied with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi).

The following steps are slightly modified based on previous literature [28]. 0.24 g OPD, 400 µL APTES, and 20 mL deionized water were added to beaker to dissolve by ultrasonication. The resulting solution was reacted in autoclave (condition: 200℃, 4h). The product achieves solid–liquid separation by centrifuge (8,000 rpm, 15 min).

0.41 g FeCl3 and 0.3 g amino terephthalic acid and 25 mL DMF were added to a beaker and sonicated until dissolved, followed by the addition of 2 mL acetic acid. The resulting solution was heated at 120 ℃ for 12 h in autoclave. To collect the precipitate, the product was centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The resulting product was dried at 70 ℃. The above steps are slightly modified based on previous literature [15].

NH2-MIL-101(Fe) aqueous dispersion (1 mg/mL) was prepared by placing 2 mg NH2-MIL-101(Fe) in a 20 mL volumetric flask and diluting it to scale with pure water. The solution A consists of 60ul NH2-MIL-101(Fe) aqueous dispersion and 80uL Si-CQDs solution. The solution A was mixed with 2 mL of different concentrations of copper ions and 1 mL of PBS solution (pH = 6.5, 10 mM) and then incubated at 80℃ for 1 h. The reaction solution was photographed with a smartphone. The fluorescence spectra were measured (excitation wavelength = 370 nm, slit width = 5 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cu2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Jinghu water sample was obtained from Jinghu Lake (Nanjing, China). The supernatant and tap water sample and Jinghu water sample were filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane. A certain concentration of copper ion solution was mixed with the real sample solution to form test solutions containing different concentrations of copper ions. 60ul of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) aqueous dispersion, 80uL of Si-CQDs solution, 2 mL of test solution and 1 mL of PBS solution (pH = 6.5, 10 mM) were mixed in a test tube, incubated and used for fluorescence testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and Si-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The diameter of NH2-MIL-101 (Fe) in SEM images ranged from 2.5 to 10 µm (Fig. 1 (a,d)). As demonstrated by Fig. 1c, the XRD patterns of the products were analyzed with the simulated MIL-101 patterns. The results show that they are basically in conformance [30]. The results of Fig. 1(e–h) showed that elements were evenly distributed in the material. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra of the products and 2-Aminoterephthalic Acid were also studied. The spectrum of 2-aminoterephthalic acid as depicted in Fig. 2 a (red curve), the peaks at 1684 and 3346 cm−1 were ascribed to the carboxyl group and amine vibrations. The spectrum of the products was depicted in Fig. 2a (blue curve). The peaks at 3454 and 3346 cm−1 were attributed to amine stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1662 and 580 cm−1 indicated stretching vibrations of the carboxyl group and the Fe–O bond [15]. The above results verified the success of the formulation of NH2-MIL-101 (Fe).

The Si-CQDs were synthesized by the reaction of o-phenylenediamine and APTES under high temperature conditions [28]. The Si-CQDs was investigated by TEM. As demonstrated by Fig. 1b, Si-CQDs showed a spherical morphology. The average particle size of Si-CQDs measured by the Malvern granulometer was 2.66 nm. Fig. 2a (black curve) displayed the FT-IR spectrum of Si-CQDs. The O-H and N-H vibration correspond to peaks at 3387 and 3360 cm−1. The C=O, and C–N vibrations correspond to peaks at 1634 and 1275 cm−1. The Si–O and Si–C vibrations correspond to peaks at 1037 and 752 cm−1 [28]. It showed that there were abundant amino and hydroxyl groups on the carbon quantum dots.

The results of the XPS examination identified the elemental composition of Si-CQD and the functional groups on its surface.As illustrated in Fig. 2b, there are four peaks in the full scan XPS spectra of Si-CQDs, which were at 102.28, 284.7, 398.98 and 532.13 eV, belonging to the binding energies of Si 2p, C1s, N1s and O1s. As demonstrated in Fig. 2c, there are peaks in the XPS spectrum of C1s, which were at 284.0, 285.03 and 285.58 eV, ascribing to C–Si, C–N and C=O [13]. The N 1 s spectrumshowed peaks at 398.63 and 400.03 eV indicating the existence of N-Si and N-H (Fig. 2d). As illustrated in Fig. 2e, the O 1 s spectrum displayed characteristic peaks at 530.68 and 532.23 eV indicating the existence of O-Si and O=C. The XPS spectrum of Si 2p displayed peaks at 101.8(Si-C), 102.2(Si-N), 102.8(Si-O) (Fig. 2f) [28]. The above proofs well confirmed the success of the formulation of Si-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of Si-CQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: The Fluorescence spectra and UV absorption spectra of Si-CQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 a(purple curve), Si-CQDs has UV absorption at both 280 and 420 nm, which severally correspond to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic ring and n-π* transitions of C=O [33].

The excitation spectrum of Si-CQDs ranged from 360 nm to 500 nm as illustrated in Fig. 3a(pink curve). Under the optimal excitation wavelength (λex = 420 nm), the Si-CQDs solution exhibited an emission peak at 563 nm. The solution of Si-CQDs shows yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation, and golden yellow under daylight (the inset in Fig. 3a). As illustrated in Fig. 3b,the as-prepared NH2-MIL-101(Fe) possess no absorption peak after 300 nm. The excitation spectrum ranged from 300 nm to 400 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was 433 nm. The NH2-MIL-101 (Fe) suspension was gray-pink in visible light and displayed blue light under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3c, Copper ions cannot cause fluorescence variation of NH2-MIL-101(Fe). However, the fluorescence peak of Si-CQDs increases after adding copper ions. The NH2-MIL-101(Fe) suspension and Si-CQDs solution were mixed to build a dual-signal response sensor. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the fluorescence emission signal peaks at 433 nm and 563 nm changed with different excitation wavelengths.

Some conditions were optimized including the volume ratio of NH2-MIL-101 (Fe) and Si-CQDs, the pH of the PBS solution, response time, and temperature. As illustrated in Fig. S2a,the fluorescence intensity ratio(F563/F433)/(F563/F433)0 of the sensor was maximum when the volume ratio is 3:4. As illustrated in Fig. S2b,when the pH of PBS solution was 6.5, the sensor showed the strongest recognition of copper ions. When the pH value is large, the performance of the sensor to detect copper ions is poor because the copper ion solution was easy to precipitate under alkaline conditions [7]. As shown in Fig. S2c, temperature can influence significantly the recognition capability of this sensor. The temperature is higher, and the recognition ability of the sensor is stronger. When the incubation temperature was 80 ℃, the sensor has the best ability to identify copper ions. When the incubation temperature was more than 80℃, the system is easy to produce black precipitation, which makes the system material metamorphic. As depicted in Fig. S2d, after incubation at 80℃ for 45 min, the value of F563/F433 were recorded, and it was found that F563/F433 was stable at 15 min, so incubation at 80℃ for one hour was selected as the best condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the sensor for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The NH2-MIL-101(Fe) suspension and Si-CQDs solution were mixed to build a fluorescence sensing platform. When the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the sensor had two emission peaks at 433 nm and 563 nm. With the increase in the concentration of copper ions, the fluorescence signal at 433 nm decreased, whereas the fluorescent intensity of Si-CQDs at 563 nm increased gradually (Fig. 4 a). Under optimal experimental conditions (VMIL-101(Fe)/VSi-CQDs = 3:4, temperature = 80℃, pH = 6.5), the sensor was applied to detect copper ions and the ratio (F563/F433) of the fluorescence signal was taken as the readout. As indicated in Fig. 4b, there was a remarkable linear correlation between F563/F433 and the concentration of copper ions in the range of 0.25–5 μM. The linear correlation equation was F563/F433 = 0.9009x + 0.3431 (R2 = 0.9985). The detection limit was 11 nM.

To further demonstrate the superiority of our constructed fluorescence sensing system, some other reported Cu2+ detection methods are compared with our method. As shown in Table 1 , many fluorescence sensors for detecting copper ions have been developed in the past three years, with very low detection limits. However, the detection mechanisms of those sensors were mostly derived from fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the sensors suffer from significant false positive interference when they are exposed to the complex matrix of real samples. In contrast, the proposed fluorescence dual-signal response sensor which was based on fluorescence enhancement mechanism in this paper was preferable in practical applications. In addition, compared to other methods, the materials do not contain toxic metals. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first smartphone-assisted colourimetric platform that can detect copper ions directly in daylight.

The selectivity was also investigated by the ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Some interfering ions, including Na+, Mg2+, Co2+, K+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ag+, and Ca2+ were added to the solution of sensor. The ratio of signals (F563/F433) was recorded under consistent conditions, as shown in Fig. 5 a. When the interfering ions exist alone, they have little interference with the signal ratio of the sensing system. When interference ions (at a concentration of 20 µM) and copper ions (at a concentration of 3 µM) co-existed, interference ions also had little effect on copper ions detection. Furthermore, Stability is the vital parameters to evaluate the analytical performance of sensors. As demonstrated by Fig. 5b, the fluorescence ratio (F563/F433) of the sensor remained unchanged within six days. However, the fluorescence ratio rises slowly on the 7th day. As indicated in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence ratio (F563/F433) of the sensor can remain stable in sodium chloride solution with a concentration ranging from 0 to 2.5 M, reflecting the good robustness of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of copper ion in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Jinghu water sample and tap water sample were used to explore the potential practicability of fluorescence sensors. According to Table 2 , the recovery rate of this method was satisfactory, with the average recovery rate ranging from 95.43 % to 103.1 %, and the relative standard deviation from 0.8 % to 3.7 %. The results show that the sensor has high accuracy and potential applicability in the copper ion analysis of water samples.

Si-CQDs were prepared using o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) by solvothermal method. In this process, 2,3-Diaminophenazine (DAP) and 2-Amino-3-hydroxyphenazine were generated. Si-CQDs were formed by the carbonization of DAP [25]. Therefore, the solution of Si-CQDs inevitably contains OPD and DAP. Meanwhile, DAP were important parts of the fluorescence source of Si-CQDs. To further investigate the mechanism, the change in the ultraviolet spectrum of the sensor after adding copper ions was studied. The UV absorption spectrum of Si-CQDs exhibited a slight absorption peak at 420 nm (Fig. 6 a). Copper ions may oxidize OPD molecules to DAP which possessed ultraviolet absorption at 420 nm and blue fluorescence signal peak at 563 nm [24,34]. Therefore, copper ions increase the UV absorption of Si-CQDs at 420 nm resulting in the addition of the overlap area with the emission spectrum (Fig. 6a).

The fluorescence signal intensity of the donor decreased, while the signal peak of Si-CQDs increased when copper ions were present in the system (Fig. 6b). The fluorescence lifetime of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was shortened from 15.09 ns to 12.87 ns because of the addition of copper ions (Fig. 6c). The results showed that copper ions were beneficial in promoting the fluorescence resonance energy transfer of the system.

We also measured the potential change of the proportional fluorescence system to investigate the mechanism. Both Si-CQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) have negative charges, especially Si-CQDs (Fig. 6d). When they coexisted, the negative charges of Si-CQDs were eliminated. The copper ions greatly reduced the negative surface potential of the constructed system, indicating that the addition of copper ions shortened the distance between NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and Si-CQDs, resulting in the enhancement of electrostatic interactions. This also confirms our previous conclusions.

In summary, copper ions oxidised the OPD group and OPD substance of the carbon quantum dots to generate DPA, which has yellow fluorescence at 563 nm. Therefore, copper ions resulted in an enhancement of the fluorescence signal at 563 nm. In the meanwhile, DPA has a UV spectrum at 420 nm, which has an overlapping area with the fluorescence spectrum of NH2-MIL-101(Fe). Thus, the addition of copper ions increased the efficiency of FRET and decreased the fluorescence intensity at 433 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of copper ions by smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: During the fluorescence assay, the reaction solution after incubation in daylight turns yellow with increasing copper ion concentration. Hence, attempts were made to combine the smartphone with the colorimetric method to detect copper ions. As displayed in the Fig. 7 (a), the reaction solution containing different concentrations of copper ions was poured into a glass bottle and then photographed with a smartphone. The corresponding RGB values were read out by the WeChat color recognition applet [27]. The value of In (B) was linearly related to the concentration of copper ions in the range of 1–5 µM, and a linear fitting equation y = −0.2646x + 5.228 (R2 = 0.9950) was obtained (Fig. 7(b)). The method is simple and convenient and does not require UV light radiation.

In this work, we successfully built a novel dual-signal response fluorescent probe based on NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and Si-CQDs to detect Cu2+ in the water sample. The probe can eliminate false positive interference. Although the incubation time is long, the sensing system can achieve ultrasensitive detection of Cu2+. The probe was applied in spiked recovery experiments and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. Moreover, the RGB value of the reaction solution was also read by smartphone, and the detection of copper ions was successfully completed. The successful construction of this optical sensor not only broadened the way to detect copper ions but also provided a new idea for the detection of other targets mediated by copper ions.

Jingjing Chen: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhixiang Li: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoqi Li: Conceptualization, Validation. Yingxiang Du: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115579.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino modification of biochar for enhanced adsorption of copper ions from synthetic wastewater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, G.-X., & Jiang, H. (2014). Amino modification of biochar for enhanced adsorption of copper ions from synthetic wastewater. Water Research, 48, 396-405. doi:10.1016/j.watres.2013.09.050

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in nano-adsorbents for the sequestration of copper from water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Emenike, E. C., Adeniyi, A. G., Omuku, P. E., Okwu, K. C., & Iwuozor, K. O. (2022). Recent advances in nano-adsorbents for the sequestration of copper from water. Journal of Water Process Engineering, 47. doi:10.1016/j.jwpe.2022.102715

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthetic fluorescent probes for studying copper in biological systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cotruvo, J. A., Jr., Aron, A. T., Ramos-Torres, K. M., & Chang, C. J. (2015). Synthetic fluorescent probes for studying copper in biological systems. Chemical Society Reviews, 44(13), 4400-4414. doi:10.1039/c4cs00346b

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microsequential injection lab-on-valve system for the spectrophotometric bi-parametric determination of iron and copper in natural waters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gonzalez, A., Mesquita, R. B. R., Avivar, J., Moniz, T., Rangel, M., Cerda, V., & Rangel, A. O. S. S. (2017). sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrothermal atomic-absorption determination of copper and zinc in mineral waters and peloids
